Journal.Title: IISE transactions on occupational ergonomics and human factors
Journal.ISOAbbreviation: IISE Trans Occup Ergon Hum Factors

Article.ArticleTitle: A Machine Learning Approach to Predict Objective and Subjective Low Back Fatigue Using Postural Control Features During Sustained Trunk Flexion.

Abstract.AbstractText: OCCUPATIONAL APPLICATIONSThis study investigated whether tree-based machine learning algorithms can predict objective (EMG-based) and subjective (Borg CR10) low back fatigue during a sustained trunk flexion, with and without back-support exosuit assistance, using postural control features. The results revealed that all tree-based algorithms reasonably predicted objective and/or subjective fatigue during sustained trunk flexion (F1-score range: 74.1%-97.7%), with Extra Trees achieving the highest mean F1-score (91.4%). Fatigue detection was more accurate with subjective or combined fatigue labeling than with objective fatigue labeling alone, and exosuit assistance improved model precision and F1-score. Feature selection identified vertical peak and mean ground reaction forces as the most influential predictors. These results suggest that noninvasive postural control monitoring via smart insoles or force-sensing shoes, combined with machine learning, can enable real-time assessment of fatigue during trunk flexion, supporting timely ergonomic interventions and workload adjustments, or exoskeleton use to mitigate the risk of low back disorders.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Human Performance Institute, Department of Industrial and Entrepreneurial Engineering and Engineering Management, Western Michigan University, Kalamazoo, MI, USA.
Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering, Northern Illinois University, DeKalb, IL, USA.
Human Performance Institute, Department of Industrial and Entrepreneurial Engineering and Engineering Management, Western Michigan University, Kalamazoo, MI, USA.

MedlineJournalInfo.MedlineTA: IISE Trans Occup Ergon Hum Factors

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: Background: Sustained trunk flexion can induce fatigue of the low back muscles, increasing the risk of low back pain. While electromyography is commonly used to quantify muscle fatigue, it is impractical in real work settings. Machine learning analysis using postural control data may provide a simple and noninvasive alternative.Purpose: This study evaluated the feasibility of tree-based machine learning algorithms to predict objective and subjective fatigue using force plate-derived features during a sustained trunk posture maintenance task, with and without back-support exosuit assistance.Methods: Eighteen participants maintained a 35° trunk flexion posture while postural control, erector spinae muscle activity, and perceived fatigue were recorded. Three fatigue labeling strategies were applied: (1) objective fatigue (≥ 12% decline in median frequency of the electromyography power spectrum), (2) subjective fatigue (Borg CR10 scale > 3), and (3) combined fatigue (both criteria met). Fifty-one time- and frequency-domain postural control features were extracted, standardized, and used to train four tree-based classifiers (Decision Tree, Random Forest, Extra Trees, XGBoost) with 10-fold cross-validation and SMOTE balancing. Statistical tests were employed to investigate the effects of labeling methods, algorithm type, and exosuit condition on model performance.Results: There was no significant effect of algorithms on fatigue predictive performance (all p > 0.272), indicating comparable performance across tree-based algorithms. In contrast, the fatigue labeling strategy significantly influenced predictive performance (all p < 0.002), with subjective and combined labeling outperforming objective fatigue labeling. Exosuit assistance improved model precision (p = 0.003) and F1-score (p = 0.011) but not accuracy or recall. Feature selection revealed that vertical ground reaction forces were the most influential predictors of fatigue in the posture studied.Conclusions: These results demonstrate the feasibility of tree-based machine learning algorithms based on postural control data to predict objective and subjective fatigue states during a sustained trunk posture maintenance task.
